(M1000) Inpatient Facilities that patient was discharged within the past 14 days?
NA Patient was not discharged from an inpatient facility
Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity (POC Element):
Edward Clyde Johnson, a 74-year-old male with a complex medical history, is being referred for home health care following a recent hospitalization and surgery for a right reverse total shoulder
arthroplasty (TSA). His diagnosis includes severe osteoarthritis of the right shoulder, along with hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, coronary artery disease with prior percutaneous coronary
intervention and stenting, and hypothyroidism. The elective procedure was performed due to longstanding shoulder pain and functional limitations that did not improve with outpatient
conservative treatment. He now requires skilled home health services to support his recovery.
In the postoperative period, Mr. Johnson has stable vital signs and controlled pain but continues to experience significant functional limitations related to the surgery. He is unable to bear weight
on his right upper extremity and needs assistance with activities of daily living such as mobility, personal care, and meal preparation. Skilled nursing services are necessary to monitor his
recovery, manage medications including pain control and anticoagulation therapy, and provide wound care to prevent complications. Physical and occupational therapy will support rehabilitation,
improve mobility, and enhance overall function. Given his age, recent surgery, and multiple comorbidities, these services are essential to promote recovery, ensure adherence to his care plan,
and reduce the risk of complications or rehospitalization.
Criteria One (One must be met): Describe what assistive devices the patient is dependent on (if any) and specifically why the patient condition warrants the special transportation or the assistance
of another person or describe the condition that makes leaving home medically contraindicated.
Criteria 1 A: Because of illness or injury
Patient needs the aid of supportive devices
Patient Needs the use of special transportation.
Patient needs the assistance of another person to leave the place of residence
Criteria 1 B: Has a condition such as leaving his or her home is medically contraindicated.
Assistance needed for safe mobility and transfer
Criteria 2 A: There must be exist a normal inability to leave home.
Unable to tolerate standing or walking for extended periods
Weakness and pain from recent surgery
Balance impaired with potential for falls; uses walls and furniture to ambulate
Cognitively impaired, unable to safely leave home without assist
Leaving home is exhausting, extended rest periods to recover
Infectious disease process causing weakness and exhaustion
Narcotics impaired ability to safely leave home unassisted
Requires human assist due to unsteady gait/poor balance
Criteria 2 B: Leaving home must require a considerable and taxing effort
Exhausting to navigate vehicle and require human assist
Leaving familiar environment causes fear and confusion
Requires assist to manage equipment and public transportation
Leaving home is strenous requiring extended rest to recover
Needs assistance to handle and transport equipment
Activities restricted due to pain limitations

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only - no surgical or
procedure codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom
control for each condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter
the ICD-10-CM code for the condition described in Column 1- no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and
sequencing requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W, X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be
reported in M1023 (Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
Column 2: the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control
for the condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W, X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control
appropriate for each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time 1 Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with
difficulty, affecting daily functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3 - Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4 - Symptoms poorly
controlled; history of re-hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are
separate items and sequencing may not coincide.
Z96.611 Presence of right artificial shoulder

Patient's current ability to prepare and take all oral medications reliably and safely, including administration of the correct dosage at the appropriate times/intervals. Excludes injectable and IV medicat
ions. (NOTE: This refers to ability, not compliance or willingness.)
0. Able to independently take the correct oral medication(s) and proper dosage(s) at the correct times.
1. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if: (a) individual dosages are prepared in advance by another person; OR (b) another person develops a drug diary or chart.
2. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if given reminders by another person at the appropriate times.
3. Unable to take medication unless administered by another person

Patient's current ability to prepare and take all prescribed injectable medications reliably and safely, including administration of correct dosage at the appropriate times/intervals. Excludes IV medicatio
0. Able to independently take the correct medication(s) and proper dosage(s) at the correct times
1. Able to take injectable medication(s) at the correct times if: a. individual syringes are prepared in advance by another person; OR b. another person develops a drug diary or
2. Able to take medication(s) at the correct times if given reminders by another person based on the frequency of the injection
3. Unable to take injectable medication unless administered by another person

Number of words repeated after first attempt
After the patient's first attempt, repeat the words using cues ( "sock, something to wear; blue, a color; bed, a piece of furniture"). You may repeat the words up to two more times.

D. Feeling tired or having little energy

and 27. Enter 99 if unable to complete interview (i.e., Symptom Frequency is blank for 3 or more required items)
How often do you feel lonely or isolated from those around you?
(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed):
1. Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2. Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3. Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4. Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5. Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7. None of the above behaviors demonstrated
(M1745) Frequency of Disruptive Behavior Symptoms (Reported or Observed):
Patient's current (day of assessment) level of alertness, orientation, comprehension, concentration, and immediate memory for simple commands
0. Alert/oriented, able to focus and shift attention, comprehends and recalls task directions independently.
1. Requires prompting (cuing, repetition, reminders) only under stressful or unfamiliar conditions.
2. Requires assistance and some direction in specific situations (for example, on all tasks involving shifting of attention) or consistently requires low stimulus environment due
3. Requires considerable assistance in routine situations. Is not alert and oriented or is unable to shift attention and recall directions more than half the time.
4. Totally dependent due to disturbances such as constant disorientation, coma, persistent vegetative state, or delirium.

Ability to see in adequate light (with glasses or other visual appliances)
0. Adequate - sees fine detail, such as regular print in newspapers/books
1. Impaired - sees large print, but not regular print in newspapers/books
2. Moderate impaired - limited vision; not able to see newspaper headlines but can identify objects
3. Highly impaired - object identification in question, but eyes appear to follow objects
4. Severely impaired - no vision or sees only light, colors or shapes; eyes do not appear to follow objects
Ability to hear (with hearing aid or hearing appliances if normally used)
0. Adequate - no difficulty in normal conversation, social interaction, listening to TV
1. Minimal difficulty - difficulty in some environments (e.g., when person speaks softly, or setting is noisy)
2. Moderate difficulty - speaker has to increase volume and speak distinctly
3. Highly impaired - absence of useful hearing
(M1400) When is the patient dyspneic or noticeably Short of Breath?
0. Patient is not short of breath
1. When walking more than 20 feet, climbing stairs
2. With moderate exertion (for example, while dressing, using commode or bedpan, walking distances less than 20 feet)
3. With minimal exertion (for example, while eating, talking, or performing other ADLs) or with agitation
Mouth and Throat (Assess oral health and swallowing ability):
Current ability to feed self meals and snacks safely. Note: This refers only to the process of eating, chewing, and swallowing, not preparing the food to be eaten.
1. Able to feed self independently but requires: (a) meal set-up; OR (b) intermittent assistance or supervision from another person; OR (c) a liquid, pureed or ground meat diet.
2. Unable to feed self and must be assisted or supervised throughout the meal/snack.
3. Able to take in nutrients orally and receives supplemental nutrients though a nasogastric tube or gastrostomy.
4. Unable to take in nutrients orally and is fed nutrients through a nasogastric tube or gastrostomy.
5. Unable to take in nutrients orally or by tube feeding.

(M1306) Does this patient have at least one Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury at Stage 2 or Higher or designated as Unstageable?
(Excludes Stage 1 pressure injuries and all healed pressure ulcers/injuries)
0. No [-> Skip to M1322, Current Number of Stage 1 Pressure Injuries at SOC/ROC; Skip to M1324, Stage of Most Problematic Unhealed Pressure
Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable at DC]
(M1311) Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage:
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact
or open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not
obscure the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often
includes undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable
pressure ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury
(M1322) Current Number of Stage 1 Pressure Injuries:
Intact skin with non blanchable redness of a localized area usually over a bony prominence. Darkly pigmented skin may not have a visible blanching; in dark skin tones only it may appear with persis
(M1324) Stage of Most Problematic Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable:
(Excludes pressure ulcer/injury that cannot be staged due to a non removable dressing/device, coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar, or deep tissue injury.)
Stage of Most Problematic: Patient has no pressure ulcers or no stageable pressure ulcers

Which of the following signs or symptoms characterize this patient as at risk for hospitalization? (Mark all that apply.)
1. History of falls (2 or more falls - or any fall with an injury - in the past 12 months)
2. Unintentional weight loss of a total of 10 pounds or more in the past 12 months
3. Multiple hospitalizations (2 or more) in the past 6 months
4. Multiple emergency department visits (2 or more) in the past 6 months
5. Decline in mental, emotional, or behavioral status in the past 3 months
6. Reported or observed history of difficulty complying with any medical instructions (for example, medications, diet, exercise) in the past 3 months
Prior history of falls within 3 months:
An unintentional change in position resulting in coming to rest on the ground or at a lower level
Inability to make it to the bathroom or commode in timely manner.
Includes but not limited to, macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathies, visual field loss, age related changes, decline in visual acuity, accommodation, glare tol
erance, depth perception, and night vision or not wearing prescribed glasses or having the correct prescription.
May include patients who need help with IADLS or ADLS or have gait or transfer problems, arthritis, pain, fear of falling, foot problems, impaired sensation, imp
aired coordination or improper use of assistive devices.
May include but not limited to, poor illumination, equipment tubing, inappropriate footwear, pets, hard to reach items, floor surfaces that are uneven or cluttered,
Poly Pharmacy (4 or more prescriptions-any type):
All PRESCRIPTIONS including prescriptions for OTC meds. Drugs highly associated with fall risk include but not limited to, sedatives, antidepressants, tranquili
zers, narcotics, antihypertensives, cardiac meds, corticosteroids, anti-anxiety drugs, anticholinergic drugs, and hypoglycemic drugs.
Pain often affects an individual's desire or ability to move or pain can be a factor in depression or compliance with safety recommendations.
Could include patients with dementia, Alzheimer's or stroke patients or patients who are confused, use poor judgment, have decreased comprehension, impulsi
vity, memory deficits. Consider patients ability to adhere to the plan of care.
A score of 4 or more is considered at risk for falling:

A. Self Care: Code the patient's need for assistance with bathing, dressing, using the toilet, or
eating prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injur
B. Indoor Mobility (Ambulation): Code the patient's need for assistance with walking from room
to room (with or without a device such as cane, crutch or walker) prior to the current illness,
C. Stairs: Code the patient's need for assistance with internal or external stairs (with or without a

D. Functional Cognition: Code the patient's need for assistance with planning regular tasks, such
as shopping or remembering to take medication prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury.
Indicate devices and aids used by the patient prior to the current illness, exacerbation, or injury (Check all that apply)
Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance - Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided throughout the activity or intermittently.
03. Partial/moderate assistance Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:

PLAN OF CARE ORDERS AND DISCHARGE PLAN
Orders and Treatment (Specify Frequency, Duration and Purpose of Visits): $484.60 (2.iv)
ADMIT PATIENT TO APLUS HOME HEALTH SERVICES. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN.
Admit patient to Home Health services for skilled nursing and physical therapy due to aftercare following joint replacement surgery, presence of right artificial shoulder joint, essential
hypertension, atherosclerotic heart disease, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, dyspnea on minimal exertion, generalized weakness, pain affecting sleep, impaired mobility, need for
assistance with transfers, fall risk, newly epithelialized surgical wound, moderate hearing impairment, dependence in ADLs, use of assistive device (cane), and use of high-risk medications
including anticoagulants and opioids. These conditions collectively limit functional ability and require continued care and support in the home setting.
SN to provide comprehensive care including cardiopulmonary assessment, pain management, medication management with attention to high-risk medications, wound care, and support with
disease management to prevent complications and promote healing. PT to evaluate and treat to improve strength, balance, and endurance, focusing on gait training, transfer techniques, fall
prevention, and increasing independence with daily activities. Skilled services are necessary to support safe recovery, reduce risk of hospitalization, and assist patient/caregiver in managing
Skilled Nurse: Once a week for one week, twice a week for one week, then once a weeks for the next two weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication review and reconciliation,
assess level of pain, infection control measures, compliance with treatment regimen and therapeutic diet, home safety and disease process teaching.
Physical Therapy: Evaluation and treatment for therapeutic exercise, strengthening, mobility, gait, transfer and fall prevention techniques training.
Home Oxygen Therapy: Continuous oxygen at 3 liters per minute via nasal cannula. SN to check O2 saturation PRN every visit for any shortness of breath on exertion, fatigue.
Patient's goal: "I want my wound to completely heal without complications; to get stronger, better and able to do the activities in daily living."
Patient Covid-19 screening completed prior to visit. No report of symptoms or known COVID-19 exposure per patient/family. Patient provided with mask for use during HH visit. Patient agreed to use
mask during treatment. Clinician performed hand hygiene, donned mask, face shield and gloves and followed infection control and bag technique procedures during the visit. No COVID related
concerns during the time of visit. Disinfection of equipment followed including dry time. Proper disposal of wastes and doffing were done upon completion of the visit.

Explained Home Health service to patient/family/caregiver(s). Explained consent to care, release of liability, and patient rights & responsibilities. Consent to care, a liability release, and consent to
photograph were signed by the patient/responsible party. A comprehensive assessment of all body systems with OASIS elements evaluation was initiated. Reconciled facility discharge medications
against routine home prescriptions without issue. Discussed advance directives, privacy policy, COVID-19 prevention, 911 protocols, basic home safety, and fall prevention principles with
patient/family/caregiver(s). Discussed the plan of care draft with the patient and explained what to expect in the next visits and the remainder of the care episode. Also explained that the initial
discussion on the management of other disease processes will be done on the next visit.
1. Patient health status and homebound reason:
Edward Clyde Johnson, a 74-year-old male with a complex medical history, is being referred for home health care following a recent hospitalization and surgery for a right reverse total shoulder
arthroplasty (TSA). His diagnosis includes severe osteoarthritis of the right shoulder, along with hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, coronary artery disease with prior percutaneous coronary
intervention and stenting, and hypothyroidism. The elective procedure was performed due to longstanding shoulder pain and functional limitations that did not improve with outpatient conservative
treatment. He now requires skilled home health services to support his recovery.

In the postoperative period, Mr. Johnson has stable vital signs and controlled pain but continues to experience significant functional limitations related to the surgery. He is unable to bear weight on
his right upper extremity and needs assistance with activities of daily living such as mobility, personal care, and meal preparation. Skilled nursing services are necessary to monitor his recovery,
manage medications including pain control and anticoagulation therapy, and provide wound care to prevent complications. Physical and occupational therapy will support rehabilitation, improve
mobility, and enhance overall function. Given his age, recent surgery, and multiple comorbidities, these services are essential to promote recovery, ensure adherence to his care plan, and reduce the
SN provided aftercare guidance for patient/caregiver following joint replacement surgery. Skilled nurse reviewed wound care and signs of infection, and explained when to report concerns. SN taught
proper use of pain medications and discussed simple comfort measures. Skilled nurse walked through safe mobility, including use of assistive devices and fall prevention, while maintaining ordered
precautions. SN also covered the importance of prescribed exercises, good nutrition, hydration, and keeping follow-up appointments to support recovery.
SN provided focused teaching for patient/caregiver regarding care with presence of a right artificial shoulder joint. Skilled nurse reviewed joint precautions and explained avoiding sudden lifting,
pushing, or reaching overhead that could strain the joint. SN taught proper positioning of the arm during rest and sleep, and discussed safe ways to perform daily activities while protecting the
shoulder. Skilled nurse walked through use of sling or support if ordered, and covered the importance of following the prescribed exercise plan to maintain strength and range of motion without
overuse. SN also highlighted signs of possible complications such as increased pain, swelling, or limited movement, and explained when to report concerns.
SN provided focused teaching for patient/caregiver regarding management of essential hypertension. Skilled nurse reviewed the importance of taking prescribed medications consistently and
explained how missed doses can affect blood pressure control. SN taught lifestyle approaches including reducing salt intake, choosing heart-healthy foods, maintaining a healthy weight, and staying
physically active as tolerated. Skilled nurse discussed limiting alcohol, avoiding smoking, and managing stress, and walked through simple ways to track blood pressure at home. SN also highlighted
warning signs such as severe headache, chest discomfort, or dizziness, and explained when to report concerns promptly.

Patient verbalized 70% of understanding on the disease process taught during this visit. Patient able to teach back 7 out of 10 about following joint replacement surgery, presence of a right artificial
shoulder joint and essential hypertension. Patient also redemonstrated 65% of home safety and fall prevention measures with the use of assistive device as per patient still demonstrates weakness,
poor endurance and slow ambulation/unsteady gait. Caregiver verbalized 75% of understanding and is available to assist patient for safety with ADLs/IADLs, medication and diet compliance for the
SOC, POC, Visit frequencies and update of patient health condition
SOC, POC, Visit frequencies and update of patient health condition
SN will continue to assess the patient's overall health condition, patient/caregiver understanding of the disease process, functional status, and knowledge of self-care management by the next visit.
HH PT for therapeutic exercise, strengthening, mobility, gait, transfer and fall prevention techniques training.

Discharge when goals met. Discharge when wound(s) healed.
Able to return to an independent level of care.

When patient knows when to notify physician.

To self care when max potential achieved

Edward C Johnson is a 74-year-old male patient referred for Home Health care on April 8, 2026, following his discharge from a short-stay acute hospital on the same day. He is under the care
of Dr. Lawrence Huff and the assessment was completed by registered nurse Julie Miller on April 9, 2026, marking the start of care. The patient prefers to communicate in English and requires
home health services to facilitate his recovery after joint replacement surgery.
The patient's homebound status is clearly defined by multiple factors that restrict his mobility. Due to his recent surgery, leaving his home is medically contraindicated, and he requires
assistance for safe mobility and transfers. He experiences dyspnea on minimal exertion, weakness, and pain, which significantly impair his mobility. Moreover, leaving home poses considerable
effort, necessitating extended rest periods for recovery due to the taxing nature of the exertion involved.
Edward's primary diagnosis is Z47.1, which pertains to aftercare following joint replacement surgery. In addition to this primary diagnosis, he has several comorbidities including Z96.611

(presence of a right artificial shoulder joint), I10 (essential hypertension), 125.10 (atherosclerotic heart disease of native coronary artery without angina pectoris), E11.9 (type 2 diabetes mellitus
without complications), and E78.5 (hyperlipidemia, unspecified). These diagnoses highlight the complexity of his medical status and indicate the need for comprehensive home health care.
A thorough head-to-toe assessment reveals that Edward exhibits a medium risk acuity level. Cardiopulmonary assessment shows a respiratory rate of 14 breaths per minute, pulse at 68 beats
per minute, and blood pressure recorded at 110/60 mmHg. He demonstrates moderate difficulty hearing and requires clear communication from caregivers. The gastrointestinal assessment
indicates he has no issues with bowel or urinary incontinence, with bowel incontinence occurring rarely. His integumentary assessment identifies a newly epithelialized surgical wound, and he
has no history of pressure ulcers.
Psychosocially, Edward lives with a non-agency caregiver who provides assistance with daily activities. His mental status is stable, as assessed by the Brief Interview for Mental Status (BIMS)
score of 15, indicating he is alert and oriented, with no signs of confusion or anxiety. He reports a good support system, which is essential for his recovery and emotional well-being.
Regarding vital signs, Edward's temperature is 98°F, and he is currently experiencing mild pain that affects his sleep. His cognitive function appears intact, as he is able to recall instructions and
perform tasks with minimal assistance. In terms of activities of daily living (ADLs), he requires support for grooming, dressing, and toileting, but he can participate in bathing with supervision.
Edward's functional ability indicates that he needs some help with self-care and indoor ambulation. He uses a cane for mobility, but he remains dependent on others for assistance during
transfers and when moving around his residence. His social needs are being addressed by the caregivers at home, and the patient has received education about emergency preparedness,
including a plan for potential disruptions in care.
A medication review reveals that Edward is currently taking multiple medications, including anticoagulants and opioids, which place him at a higher risk for adverse effects. He has been
educated on the importance of compliance with his medication regimen and the risks associated with his high-risk medications. There are no indications of ineffective drug therapy or significant
Edward has a primary caregiver who assists him with daily tasks and is aware of his medical history and care needs. This support network is crucial for his recovery, particularly given his recent
surgery and ongoing health concerns. The patient is also encouraged to engage in nutritional practices aligned with his therapeutic diet, which includes low salt, low fat, and controlled
carbohydrates to manage his diabetes and hypertension effectively.
In conclusion, the medical necessity for skilled services in home health care is evident. Edward requires regular nursing assessments, medication management, assistance with daily activities,
and support for mobility and rehabilitation following his joint replacement surgery. The recommended visit frequency is at least two to three times per week to ensure he receives the necessary
care and monitoring for his recovery process.
